title: Types of Controls
subtitle: Prevent, detect, correct — and how

hook.metaphor: Think about protecting a house. A lock stops the burglar getting in (prevent). An alarm tells you if they do (detect). Insurance and a locksmith help you recover afterwards (correct). You wouldn't rely on just one — and you'd automate what you can (a smart lock beats remembering to lock up). Controls come in exactly these flavours.
hook.scenario: A finance team is proud that every payment needs manager approval. But the approver rubber-stamps 200 a day without looking, and nobody ever reviews the payments afterward. They have one type of control, done weakly, and nothing to back it up. Where are the gaps?

learn: By **timing**, controls are **preventive** (stop the bad thing before it happens — approvals, access limits, validation), **detective** (catch it quickly after — reconciliations, exception reports, monitoring), or **corrective** (fix and recover — backups, incident response, clawbacks). A healthy system has all three; prevention always leaks, so you need detection and recovery too.

By **how they run**, controls are **manual** (a person does it — flexible but error-prone and tiring, like our rubber-stamping approver) or **automated** (a system enforces it — consistent, scalable, and harder to bypass). Automate the repetitive, high-volume checks; reserve human judgement for the genuinely judgemental.

By **importance**, some are **key controls** — the few that really matter, where failure would be serious — and others are secondary. When a key control can't operate, a **compensating control** stands in to cover the gap. You spend your attention and assurance on the key ones.

The art is the **mix**. Our payment team has a single preventive control (approval), executed so weakly it's theatre, with no detective backstop. Better: tighten the approval (only large/unusual payments need human eyes), automate validation, and add a detective review of a sample afterwards. Layered, varied controls beat one heroic checkpoint every time.
bridge: So at work, you can pressure-test any control setup with two questions: *Do we have prevention, detection, AND correction — or are we betting everything on one?* and *Could this be automated to be more reliable than a tired human?* A single manual checkpoint, however well-meant, is the most common and most fragile control pattern there is.

interactions.title: Prevent, detect, or correct?
interactions.prompt: Sort each control by when it acts.

items.label: Access limits so only authorised staff can pay
A daily reconciliation flagging odd payments
Restoring data from backup after an error
Requiring approval before a large transfer
An exception report reviewed each morning

interactions.title: Fix the rubber-stamp
interactions.prompt: The manager approves 200 payments a day without looking, with no after-the-fact review. Best improvement?

options.label: Keep it — at least there's an approval step.
options.feedback: An approval nobody actually performs is theatre, not a control. On paper it exists; in reality it stops nothing. This is the documented-vs-implemented trap.

options.label: Automate routine checks, route only unusual payments to humans, add a sample review after.
options.feedback: Exactly. You make the preventive control meaningful (humans see only what needs judgement), let automation handle volume, and add a detective backstop. Layered and realistic.

options.label: Add a second rubber-stamping approver.
options.feedback: Two people not looking is no better than one. Adding more of a broken manual control just doubles the theatre.

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Controls come in three timings — prevent, detect, correct — and two styles — manual or automated — and a good system layers a mix rather than betting everything on one tired checkpoint.”

checkpoint.q: A reconciliation that catches an error the next day is a… control.
checkpoint.explain: Detective controls catch problems quickly after they occur; preventive ones stop them up front.

checkpoint.q: Why prefer automated controls for high-volume, repetitive checks?
options: They're consistent, scalable, and harder to bypass than tired humans
checkpoint.explain: Automation beats manual effort on consistency and volume; save human judgement for genuinely judgemental calls.

options: Stands in to cover the gap when a key control can't operate
Replaces the need for any controls
checkpoint.explain: When a key control fails or can't run, a compensating control covers the same risk another way.
